quiz_questions.question_text: Asthma is characterized by:

options.text: Permanent, irreversible airway narrowing
Reversible airway inflammation, bronchospasm, and mucus hypersecretion
Permanent destruction of alveolar walls
Scarring and fibrosis of the lung interstitium

quiz_questions.explanation: The hallmark of asthma is reversibility: airway inflammation, smooth muscle spasm, and excess mucus can be relieved with bronchodilators and anti-inflammatory medications, distinguishing it from the irreversible obstruction seen in COPD.

quiz_questions.question_text: The number one risk factor for developing COPD is:

options.text: Genetics alone
Chronic cigarette smoking
Seasonal allergies
Viral infections in childhood

quiz_questions.explanation: Chronic cigarette smoking is responsible for approximately 80-90% of COPD cases. Tobacco smoke causes chronic airway inflammation, mucus gland hypertrophy, and progressive destruction of lung tissue over years to decades.

quiz_questions.question_text: Emphysema specifically involves:

options.text: Excess mucus production in the bronchi
Irreversible destruction of alveolar walls, reducing surface area for gas exchange and causing loss of elastic recoil
Fluid accumulation in the alveoli
Reversible bronchospasm

quiz_questions.explanation: In emphysema, proteolytic enzymes (especially neutrophil elastase) destroy the alveolar septae, creating large, inefficient air spaces. This reduces gas exchange surface area and destroys the elastic fibers needed for passive expiration, causing air trapping.

quiz_questions.question_text: Chronic bronchitis is clinically defined as:

options.text: A single episode of bronchial infection lasting 2 weeks
A productive cough occurring most days for at least 3 months in 2 consecutive years
Destruction of alveolar walls with air trapping
Acute bacterial infection of the bronchial tree

quiz_questions.explanation: Chronic bronchitis is defined clinically by a productive cough (with sputum production) present most days of the month for at least 3 months per year in two consecutive years. It results from chronic airway inflammation with goblet cell hypertrophy and excess mucus.

quiz_questions.question_text: Pneumonia involves infection of the:

options.text: Pleural membranes only
Lung parenchyma (alveoli) by bacteria, viruses, or fungi
Trachea and main bronchi only
Nasal sinuses only

quiz_questions.explanation: Pneumonia is an infection of the lung parenchyma in which the alveoli become filled with inflammatory exudate, pus, and fluid. It can be caused by bacteria (e.g., Streptococcus pneumoniae), viruses (e.g., influenza), or fungi (e.g., Pneumocystis).

quiz_questions.question_text: Tuberculosis (TB) is caused by:

options.text: A virus
Mycobacterium tuberculosis (an acid-fast bacterium)
A fungus
A parasite

quiz_questions.explanation: TB is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, an acid-fast bacillus transmitted through airborne droplets. The bacteria can form granulomas in the lungs and may remain dormant (latent TB) for years before reactivating.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pulmonary embolism (PE) most commonly originates from:

options.text: A blood clot formed in the pulmonary arteries
A deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in the lower extremities
A clot in the brain
An arterial clot in the coronary arteries

quiz_questions.explanation: Most pulmonary emboli originate from deep venous thrombi in the legs or pelvis. A portion of the thrombus breaks free (embolizes), travels through the right side of the heart, and lodges in the pulmonary arterial vasculature, blocking blood flow to the affected lung region.

quiz_questions.question_text: Lung cancer is most strongly associated with:

options.text: Regular exercise
Cigarette smoking (responsible for ~85% of cases)
A high-protein diet
Cold weather exposure

quiz_questions.explanation: Cigarette smoking is the leading risk factor for lung cancer, accounting for approximately 85% of all cases. The carcinogens in tobacco smoke cause DNA mutations in bronchial epithelial cells that can lead to uncontrolled cell growth over years of exposure.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cystic fibrosis is caused by:

options.text: A bacterial infection acquired in childhood
A genetic mutation in the CFTR gene, producing thick, sticky mucus in the lungs and other organs
An allergic reaction to environmental pollutants
Chronic cigarette smoking

quiz_questions.explanation: Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive genetic disorder caused by mutations in the CFTR (cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator) gene. The defective chloride channel leads to abnormally thick, dehydrated mucus that obstructs airways and promotes chronic lung infections.

quiz_questions.question_text: An SpO₂ reading below _____ generally indicates clinically significant hypoxemia.
quiz_questions.explanation: An SpO₂ below 90% corresponds approximately to a PaO₂ below 60 mmHg on the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve, which marks the point where further small drops in PO₂ cause significant desaturation. This threshold generally warrants supplemental oxygen.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is COPD?

options.text: A reversible airway disease characterized by acute bronchospasm
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease — a progressive, largely irreversible condition of airflow limitation that includes chronic bronchitis and emphysema
A genetic disease causing thick mucus production
An acute lung infection caused by bacteria

quiz_questions.explanation: COPD is an umbrella term for chronic bronchitis and emphysema, both characterized by progressive, poorly reversible airflow limitation. The disease is usually caused by long-term exposure to lung irritants, most commonly cigarette smoke.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is ARDS (Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome)?

options.text: A mild allergic reaction in the airways
A life-threatening condition of severe, diffuse lung inflammation causing bilateral pulmonary edema, impaired gas exchange, and respiratory failure
A chronic obstructive lung disease caused by smoking
A bacterial pneumonia limited to one lobe

quiz_questions.explanation: ARDS is a severe, acute inflammatory response in the lungs triggered by conditions such as sepsis, pneumonia, trauma, or aspiration. Massive capillary leak floods the alveoli with protein-rich fluid, producing refractory hypoxemia that requires ICU-level care and mechanical ventilation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a pleural effusion?

options.text: Air accumulation in the pleural space
Abnormal accumulation of excess fluid in the pleural space between the visceral and parietal pleura
Inflammation of the bronchial tubes
A blood clot in the pulmonary arteries

quiz_questions.explanation: A pleural effusion is the pathological accumulation of fluid in the pleural space. It can be transudative (from pressure changes, as in heart failure) or exudative (from inflammation, as in infection or malignancy), and large effusions can compress the lung and impair breathing.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is atelectasis?

options.text: Permanent destruction of alveolar walls
Collapse or closure of a lung or part of a lung, resulting in reduced gas exchange in the affected area
Infection of the lung tissue with bacteria
Excess fluid in the pleural space

quiz_questions.explanation: Atelectasis refers to the partial or complete collapse of lung tissue. It can be caused by airway obstruction (obstructive atelectasis), external compression (compressive atelectasis from pleural effusion or tumor), or loss of surfactant. It is common after surgery.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is pulmonary fibrosis?

options.text: Reversible airway obstruction from bronchospasm
Progressive scarring and thickening of lung tissue that reduces compliance and impairs gas exchange
Acute bacterial infection of the alveoli
Destruction of alveolar walls from protease activity

quiz_questions.explanation: Pulmonary fibrosis involves the progressive replacement of normal lung tissue with scar tissue (collagen). The fibrotic lung becomes stiff (low compliance) and the thickened interstitium increases diffusion distance, resulting in a restrictive ventilatory pattern and impaired gas exchange.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'hemoptysis' mean?

options.text: Vomiting blood from the stomach
Coughing up blood or bloody sputum originating from the respiratory tract
Blood in the urine
Nosebleed caused by nasal irritation

quiz_questions.explanation: Hemoptysis (hemo- = blood, -ptysis = spitting) is the coughing up of blood from the lungs or bronchial tree. It can range from blood-streaked sputum to massive hemorrhage and may indicate serious conditions such as lung cancer, tuberculosis, or pulmonary embolism.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'bronchospasm' mean?

options.text: Permanent narrowing of the airways due to scar tissue
Sudden constriction of the smooth muscle in the bronchial walls, causing airway narrowing and difficulty breathing
Dilation of the airways in response to exercise
Collapse of the alveoli from surfactant deficiency

quiz_questions.explanation: Bronchospasm is an involuntary contraction of the smooth muscle surrounding the bronchi and bronchioles, causing sudden narrowing of the airways. It is a key feature of asthma attacks and anaphylaxis, producing wheezing, coughing, and difficulty breathing (especially exhaling).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is consolidation on a chest X-ray?

options.text: Normal air-filled lung appearing dark on X-ray
An area of lung where the alveoli are filled with fluid, pus, blood, or cells, appearing as a white opacity on the X-ray, commonly seen in pneumonia
A collapsed lung with no air present
Increased air in the lungs appearing hyper-dark

quiz_questions.explanation: Consolidation refers to lung tissue that has become solidified because the normally air-filled alveoli are filled with material (inflammatory exudate, pus, or fluid). On a chest X-ray, consolidated areas appear as dense white (opaque) regions against the normally dark, air-filled lung.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'obstructive lung disease' mean?

options.text: A disease where the lungs cannot expand fully due to stiffness
A category of lung diseases (asthma, COPD) characterized by difficulty exhaling due to increased airway resistance, causing air trapping
A disease where blood flow to the lungs is blocked
A disease caused by obstruction of the trachea by a foreign body

quiz_questions.explanation: Obstructive lung diseases are characterized by increased resistance to airflow, particularly during expiration. The hallmark spirometric finding is a reduced FEV₁/FVC ratio (<70%), indicating that the patient cannot exhale air quickly due to narrowed or collapsing airways.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'restrictive lung disease' mean?

options.text: A disease characterized by difficulty exhaling due to airway narrowing
A category of lung diseases (pulmonary fibrosis, scoliosis) where the lungs or chest wall cannot expand fully, reducing lung volumes
A disease caused by excess surfactant production
A disease where the airways are blocked by mucus

quiz_questions.explanation: Restrictive lung diseases limit lung expansion, resulting in reduced total lung capacity and forced vital capacity. They can be caused by intrinsic lung conditions (fibrosis, ARDS) or extrinsic factors (chest wall deformity, neuromuscular disease, obesity). The FEV₁/FVC ratio is typically normal or increased.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 45-year-old woman who has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 25 years presents with worsening shortness of breath and a chronic productive cough. Spirometry shows FEV₁/FVC of 58%. The physician diagnoses COPD. Which combination of pathological changes best explains her symptoms?

options.text: Reversible bronchospasm and temporary mucus production
Chronic airway inflammation with excess mucus (chronic bronchitis) combined with alveolar wall destruction and loss of elastic recoil (emphysema), causing irreversible airflow obstruction
Scarring and thickening of the lung interstitium reducing compliance
Acute bacterial infection of the lung parenchyma

quiz_questions.explanation: COPD typically involves elements of both chronic bronchitis (inflamed, mucus-producing airways) and emphysema (destroyed alveoli with lost elastic recoil). The combined effect of increased airway resistance and loss of expiratory driving force creates the progressive, largely irreversible airflow limitation that defines COPD.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 30-year-old teacher with a history of asthma is rushed to the ER after exposure to a classroom allergen. She is wheezing severely and cannot complete a sentence. Her peak flow is 35% of predicted. The emergency physician administers nebulized albuterol and intravenous corticosteroids. After 20 minutes, her peak flow improves to 75%. What does this improvement demonstrate about the nature of asthma?

options.text: The alveolar damage from asthma was surgically repaired
Asthma is characterized by reversible airway obstruction — bronchodilators relax the constricted smooth muscle and steroids reduce inflammation, restoring airflow
The medications cured her asthma permanently
The peak flow meter was malfunctioning initially

quiz_questions.explanation: The dramatic improvement with bronchodilator therapy demonstrates asthma's defining feature: reversibility. Unlike COPD, where airflow limitation is largely fixed, asthma's bronchospasm, airway edema, and mucus plugging can be substantially reversed with appropriate treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 72-year-old man who recently had hip replacement surgery suddenly develops severe shortness of breath, sharp chest pain, and a rapid heart rate on postoperative day 3. CT angiography reveals a large pulmonary embolism in the right pulmonary artery. Based on pulmonary pathophysiology, why does a PE cause such acute respiratory distress?

options.text: The clot blocks all airflow to the right lung
The clot obstructs blood flow to a lung region, creating dead space ventilation and V/Q mismatch, while also increasing pulmonary vascular resistance and straining the right heart
The clot causes the alveoli to fill with pus
The clot releases toxins that paralyze the diaphragm

quiz_questions.explanation: A PE blocks perfusion to the affected lung region while ventilation continues (dead space). This V/Q mismatch impairs gas exchange. Additionally, the obstruction increases pulmonary vascular resistance, which can cause acute right heart failure, further compromising cardiac output and oxygen delivery.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5-year-old boy with cystic fibrosis is hospitalized for a pulmonary exacerbation. His sputum is thick, green, and difficult to clear. Cultures grow Pseudomonas aeruginosa. The pulmonologist orders aggressive chest physiotherapy and IV antibiotics. Why are patients with CF so susceptible to chronic lung infections?

options.text: CF patients lack an immune system
The defective CFTR channel produces abnormally thick, dehydrated mucus that cannot be effectively cleared, creating an environment where bacteria thrive and the mucociliary escalator fails
CF destroys the alveoli, eliminating the gas exchange surface
CF causes the trachea to narrow permanently

quiz_questions.explanation: In CF, the defective CFTR chloride channel leads to inadequate chloride and water secretion onto airway surfaces. The resulting thick, sticky mucus overwhelms the mucociliary clearance mechanism, creating stagnant mucus pools where bacteria (especially Pseudomonas) establish chronic biofilm infections.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 60-year-old retired construction worker presents with progressive dyspnea over the past 2 years. He has never smoked. His chest CT shows diffuse interstitial fibrosis. Spirometry reveals reduced FVC with a normal FEV₁/FVC ratio. Pulmonary function tests confirm a restrictive pattern. His occupational history reveals decades of asbestos exposure. What type of lung disease is this?

options.text: Obstructive lung disease (like COPD)
Restrictive lung disease (asbestosis) — the fibrotic, stiff lungs cannot expand fully, reducing all lung volumes while maintaining a normal FEV₁/FVC ratio
Reversible asthma triggered by occupational exposure
Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)

quiz_questions.explanation: Asbestosis is a type of pulmonary fibrosis caused by chronic asbestos inhalation. The scarring makes the lungs stiff (reduced compliance), producing a restrictive pattern on spirometry: reduced FVC and TLC with a preserved or elevated FEV₁/FVC ratio, because the stiff lungs can actually empty rapidly.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 55-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use is found aspirating vomitus. He is intubated and admitted to the ICU. Over the next 24 hours, he develops severe bilateral infiltrates on chest X-ray, refractory hypoxemia (PaO₂ of 50 mmHg on 100% FiO₂), and requires high ventilator pressures. The intensivist diagnoses ARDS. What distinguishes ARDS from ordinary pneumonia?

options.text: ARDS affects only one lobe, while pneumonia is always bilateral
ARDS involves diffuse, bilateral inflammation with widespread capillary leak and non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, causing refractory hypoxemia that responds poorly to supplemental oxygen alone
ARDS is always caused by bacteria, while pneumonia is always viral
ARDS is a mild condition that resolves without treatment

quiz_questions.explanation: ARDS is characterized by widespread, diffuse alveolar damage with bilateral pulmonary edema from capillary leak (not heart failure). The extensive alveolar flooding creates massive intrapulmonary shunting, causing severe hypoxemia that does not correct well with supplemental O₂ alone, requiring mechanical ventilation with positive pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient is diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia. His chest X-ray shows right lower lobe consolidation. Blood cultures and sputum cultures are sent, and the physician starts empiric antibiotics immediately without waiting for results. A medical student asks why treatment begins before the causative organism is identified. What is the rationale?

options.text: Culture results are never useful in pneumonia management

Delaying antibiotics until culture results return (24-72 hours) could allow the infection to worsen; empiric antibiotics cover the most likely pathogens and can be adjusted once culture and sensitivity results are available
The physician randomly selected an antibiotic because all antibiotics work equally well
Cultures cannot identify the bacteria causing pneumonia

quiz_questions.explanation: Empiric antibiotic therapy is started immediately because culture results take 24-72 hours. The initial antibiotic choice is guided by the most probable pathogens (e.g., S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae) based on clinical setting. Once culture and sensitivity data return, therapy can be narrowed to target the specific organism.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physician tells a long-term smoker that quitting now, even after 30 years, would still provide significant health benefits. The patient is skeptical and asks if it is 'too late.' Based on respiratory disease prevention, what is the evidence-based response?

options.text: It is indeed too late — the damage is entirely permanent
Smoking cessation at any age reduces the risk of lung cancer, slows COPD progression, improves cardiovascular health, and increases life expectancy — it is never too late to benefit
Only smokers under age 30 benefit from quitting
Quitting only helps if the smoker has fewer than 10 pack-years

quiz_questions.explanation: Evidence consistently shows that smoking cessation at any age provides measurable health benefits. The rate of lung function decline slows, cancer risk gradually decreases (though it remains elevated compared to never-smokers), and cardiovascular risk drops significantly within the first year of quitting.

quiz_questions.question_text: During a COVID-19 surge, a 58-year-old patient is admitted with bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT, worsening hypoxemia, and a diagnosis of COVID-19 pneumonia progressing to ARDS. Despite high-flow oxygen, his SpO₂ remains at 80%. The pulmonologist decides to intubate and initiate prone positioning. Why does prone positioning (lying face down) help oxygenation in ARDS?

options.text: It allows the patient to cough up secretions more easily
Prone positioning redistributes ventilation to better-perfused dorsal lung regions, improving V/Q matching and reducing shunt fraction
It reduces the production of inflammatory cytokines
It compresses the lungs to squeeze out excess fluid

quiz_questions.explanation: In ARDS, the dependent (dorsal) lung regions tend to collapse under the weight of the edematous lung. Turning the patient prone redistributes lung perfusion and opens collapsed dorsal alveoli, improving V/Q matching. Studies have shown that prone positioning improves oxygenation and reduces mortality in severe ARDS.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 40-year-old woman presents with gradually worsening shortness of breath. She has no history of smoking. Her spirometry shows FVC of 55% predicted with FEV₁/FVC of 85%. Her physician orders additional testing and finds significantly elevated alpha-1 antitrypsin levels are low. What is the connection between alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency and lung disease?

options.text: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency causes excessive mucus production blocking the airways
Without sufficient alpha-1 antitrypsin to inhibit neutrophil elastase, the protease destroys alveolar walls, leading to early-onset emphysema even in non-smokers
Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency prevents surfactant production
The deficiency weakens the diaphragm and intercostal muscles

quiz_questions.explanation: Alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) is a protease inhibitor that normally protects lung tissue from neutrophil elastase. When AAT is deficient (genetic condition), unopposed elastase activity progressively destroys alveolar walls, producing panacinar emphysema at a much younger age than smoking-related emphysema, even in people who have never smoked.
